hook: In single-variable calculus, you mastered how functions change along a line — but the real world rarely works in just one dimension. A mountain's elevation depends on both latitude and longitude, and a weather system's pressure depends on position, altitude, and time. How do we measure rates of change, find peaks and valleys, and optimize systems when many variables are pulling at once?
concept: This unit extends the core ideas of derivatives to functions of two or more variables, revealing how to measure, approximate, and optimize change in multiple directions simultaneously — captured formally by the concept of the *total derivative* and the gradient vector.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Functions of Several Variables and Level Curves
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to interpret and visualize functions like $f(x, y)$ using graphs, tables, and level curves — the same technique used in topographic maps and weather charts.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Limits and Continuity in Several Variables (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Master how to evaluate limits as $(x, y) \to (a, b)$ and discover why limits in higher dimensions are trickier — a point can be approached from infinitely many paths.

Apply the familiar derivative rule one variable at a time to find $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ and $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$, measuring how a function changes when you move in a single coordinate direction.
You will learn to construct the tangent plane to a surface at a point and use it as a linear approximation $L(x, y)$ — the multivariable analogue of the tangent line.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Chain Rule for Multivariable Functions
topic_roadmap.description: Master the multivariable Chain Rule to differentiate composite functions like $f(x(t), y(t))$ and navigate dependencies between variables using tree diagrams.

You will learn to compute the rate of change of $f$ in any direction using the directional derivative, and discover that the gradient vector $\nabla f$ always points in the direction of steepest ascent.
Apply the Second Derivative Test for functions of two variables to classify critical points as local maxima, minima, or saddle points on a surface.
Master the method of Lagrange multipliers to optimize a function $f(x, y)$ subject to a constraint $g(x, y) = k$ — a powerful technique used in economics, engineering, and machine learning.
